message.content:
Hijack Execution Flow involves adversaries manipulating the way operating systems run programs to execute malicious payloads. This technique can be used for persistence, privilege escalation, or evading defenses like application control. Adversaries may hijack execution flow by altering how the OS locates and loads programs or libraries, often by poisoning file directories or the Windows Registry with malicious payloads. Examples include replacing non-existent Windows DLL files with malicious versions or using tools like Dtrack and ShimRat to alter program execution flow, escalate privileges, and bypass User Account Control (UAC).

Potential impacts include unauthorized access, persistent malware presence, and compromised system integrity. Common exploitation methods involve DLL hijacking, registry manipulation, and file path interception.

Mitigation strategies include implementing application whitelisting, monitoring and restricting changes to critical system directories and the registry, and employing robust endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious activities. Regular system audits and integrity checks can also help identify and remediate hijacked execution flows.
